content_markdown:
The electromagnetic spectrum radiated by a black body at temperature $T$ shows a peak at wavelength $\lambda_p$.

What is the variation of $\lambda_p$ with $T$?

diagram_metadata.description: Four graphs showing different relationships between peak wavelength lambda_p on the y-axis and temperature T on the x-axis.

options.label: A
options.text: A graph showing an inverse relationship (hyperbola) where $\lambda_p$ decreases as $T$ increases.

options.label: B
options.text: A graph showing a linear relationship with a positive gradient and a positive y-intercept.

options.label: C
options.text: A graph showing a linear relationship with a negative gradient.

options.label: D
options.text: A graph showing a curve that starts at the origin, rises to a peak, and then decreases (resembling a Planck curve shape).

vibe_explanation: According to Wien's displacement law, the peak wavelength $\lambda_p$ is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature $T$ of the black body ($\lambda_p \propto \frac{1}{T}$). This relationship is represented by a rectangular hyperbola, which is shown in graph A.
